expectations.text: Detects or asks about the test framework before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Phase 1 (Framework Detection) runs before any Test Writer is spawned. Trace lines 111-147 show the orchestrator auto-detecting TypeScript + Vitest from package.json/tsconfig.json, producing a complete framework config (language, testRunner, testCommand, testFilePattern, sourceDir, testDir) that is stored in the state file and used by all subsequent agents. This occurs before Phase 2 (Decomposition) and well before Phase 4 (Agent Team Orchestration). The detected framework info is also shown to the user in the confirmation prompt (line 194: 'Framework: vitest (auto-detected)').

expectations.text: Presents a work decomposition plan and asks for user confirmation before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Phase 2 (lines 150-218) decomposes the spec into three work units (capitalize, truncate, slugify) with explicit specs, file paths, and dependency analysis. Lines 187-216 show the orchestrator presenting a formatted plan ('## TDD Work Plan') including framework, mode, unit details, dependencies, and execution strategy, ending with 'Proceed? [confirm/modify/cancel]'. Line 218 states 'User confirms. Execution proceeds.' No agent work begins until after confirmation.

expectations.text: Test Writer creates test files BEFORE any implementation code exists
expectations.evidence: For each unit, the Test Writer (Step 4a) runs first and creates only test files and spec-contract files. For Unit 1 (capitalize), the Test Writer produces capitalize.test.ts (lines 347-386) before any implementation exists. The RED verification (Step 4b) confirms this ordering: tests fail because 'src/string-utils.ts does not exist' (line 437) or has 'no exported member' (lines 815-816 for truncate, line 967 for slugify). The Code Writer (Step 4c) only runs after RED verification passes. The skill template explicitly instructs the Test Writer: 'WRITE TESTS ONLY. Do not create implementation files' (line 326).

expectations.text: Tests fail initially (RED phase verified) -- the skill explicitly checks for non-zero exit code
expectations.evidence: RED verification is performed for all three units. For capitalize (lines 422-484): the test command includes 'echo EXIT_CODE:$?' (line 435), output shows 'EXIT_CODE:1' (line 445), and the trace explicitly states 'Exit code is 1 (non-zero). Tests FAIL. This is the expected RED state. PASS.' For truncate (lines 807-828): 'EXIT_CODE:1. PASS.' For slugify (lines 963-976): 'Tests fail with import error. EXIT_CODE:1. PASS.' Additionally, Check 3 verifies the failure type is acceptable (import/module error, not syntax error), and Check 4 verifies assertion density.

expectations.text: Code Writer receives test file contents read from disk, not from Test Writer output (information barrier)
expectations.evidence: Lines 487-548 describe the information barrier for Unit 1 in detail. Line 491: 'The orchestrator reads from DISK (not from Test Writer output): 1. Test file contents: reads src/__tests__/capitalize.test.ts from disk 2. Spec-contract: reads src/__tests__/spec-contract-capitalize.md from disk.' A Lead Verification Checklist (lines 494-499) confirms: prompt contains test file contents read from DISK, prompt contains spec-contract read from DISK, prompt does NOT contain any Test Writer prompt text, prompt does NOT contain references to 'Test Writer', 'Agent A', or test authoring. Lines 543-548 enumerate what the Code Writer does NOT receive. For Units 2 and 3, lines 831 and 979 reaffirm 'Information barrier enforced.'

expectations.text: Code Writer does NOT modify test files (checksum verification mentioned or performed)
expectations.evidence: Checksums are recorded during RED verification (lines 472-478 for capitalize, line 827 for truncate, line 975 for slugify). During GREEN verification, Check 1 explicitly compares checksums: for capitalize (lines 569-575) 'Checksums match. The Code Writer did not modify test files. PASS.' For truncate (line 846) 'Checksum matches. PASS.' For slugify (line 994) 'Checksum matches. PASS.' Additionally, Check 2 scans for skip/focus markers that could subvert tests (lines 577-583, 847, 995).

expectations.text: All tests pass after implementation (GREEN phase)
expectations.evidence: GREEN verification Check 3 runs the test command and verifies EXIT_CODE:0 for each unit. Capitalize: lines 586-607 show all 8 tests passing with EXIT_CODE:0. Truncate: line 852 'All 10 tests pass. EXIT_CODE:0. PASS.' Slugify: line 996 'All 12 tests pass. EXIT_CODE:0. PASS.' Phase 5 (Final Review, lines 1042-1074) runs the full test suite together: 'Tests: 30 passed EXIT_CODE:0' and the orchestrator 'reads the actual output -- does not assume success' (line 1064).

expectations.text: An adversarial review step is performed on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Adversarial review (Step 4f) is performed for all three units. Capitalize: lines 676-742 show a full adversarial review by a sonnet-class model checking Test Completeness (4/5), Test Quality (5/5), Implementation Quality (4/5), Cheating Detection (CLEAN), and Coverage Gaps. Truncate: lines 862-894 show a complete review with all 5/5 scores and CLEAN cheating detection. Slugify: lines 1005-1038 show another full review with specific findings about Unicode handling. Each review follows the adversarial-reviewer-prompt.md template and checks for anti-patterns from testing-anti-patterns.md.

expectations.text: A tdd-report.md or equivalent summary is generated at the end
expectations.evidence: Phase 6 (lines 1105-1197) generates tdd-report.md with a complete session report. Lines 1109-1193 show the full report content including: date, spec, framework, entry point, summary table (work units, tests, assertions, anti-cheat violations, adversarial reviews, retries), detailed per-unit phase tables, anti-cheat log, and final integration check results. The report is listed as a persisted deliverable in the files table (line 1249: 'tdd-report.md | Session report (deliverable) | Yes (not gitignored)'). A tdd-session.jsonl event log is also generated (line 1197).

expectations.text: The skill does NOT skip any verification steps even for simple specs
expectations.evidence: Despite all three units being classified as 'Simple' complexity (line 65), every verification step runs for every unit: RED verification with all 5 checks (tests exist, tests fail, correct failure type, assertion density, behavior-over-implementation) plus checksum recording; GREEN verification with all 4 checks (test files unchanged via checksum, no skip/focus markers, all tests pass, no hardcoded returns); spec compliance review; adversarial review; and a final integration check (Phase 5) running all 30 tests together. The only step skipped is the Design Gate (Phase 0), which is a planning/architecture step, not a verification step -- and the trace explains this is because the spec describes simple stateless pure functions with no ambiguity (lines 87-107). The model strategy assigns 'haiku' for simpler agent roles but 'sonnet' for reviewers (lines 66-70), showing that even cost optimization does not reduce verification rigor.

expectations.text: Detects or asks about the test framework before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 2-3: Claude inspects project context and picks a language/framework ad-hoc based on general heuristics. The trace states 'Claude picks a framework ad-hoc without systematic detection' and section 'Does NOT Detect Test Frameworks Systematically' confirms no systematic scan for existing test configurations across the project. No user query about framework preference occurs.

expectations.text: Presents a work decomposition plan and asks for user confirmation before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1: Claude identifies three functions but treats them as a single implementation task. Section 'Does NOT Decompose Into Work Units' states: 'Default Claude treats the three functions as a single implementation task. It does not decompose the spec into independent work units.' Section 'Does NOT Enforce a Design Gate' confirms: 'It jumps straight from parsing the request to writing the implementation.' No user confirmation step exists.

expectations.text: Test Writer creates test files BEFORE any implementation code exists
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 4 explicitly states: 'This is the critical divergence from TDD. Default Claude Code writes the implementation immediately. No tests exist.' Step 5 says tests are written AFTER implementation ('maybe'). Section 'Does NOT Write Tests First (No RED Phase)' confirms: 'The implementation is written before any test exists.'

expectations.text: Tests fail initially (RED phase verified) -- the skill explicitly checks for non-zero exit code
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Verify Tests Fail Before Implementation (No RED/GREEN Cycle)' states: 'Default Claude skips all three phases. Tests and implementation are produced in one shot or in implementation-first order. There is no verification that the tests would have failed without the implementation.' No non-zero exit code check occurs at any point.

expectations.text: Code Writer receives test file contents read from disk, not from Test Writer output (information barrier)
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Use Agent Teams or Information Barriers' states: 'Default Claude operates as a single agent in a single context. It does not spawn a Code Writer agent that receives tests from disk (not from the test-writing context). Everything happens in one context, so the entity writing tests has full knowledge of (or has just written) the implementation.'

expectations.text: Code Writer does NOT modify test files (checksum verification mentioned or performed)
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Use Agent Teams or Information Barriers' states: 'It does not enforce that the Code Writer cannot modify test files. It does not use checksum verification to detect test tampering.' Single agent writes both tests and implementation with no separation or integrity checks.

expectations.text: All tests pass after implementation (GREEN phase)
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 6 says tests would pass: 'Since the tests were written to match the implementation, they will pass.' However, there is no distinct GREEN phase -- tests were never verified to fail first, so the pass is not a meaningful RED-to-GREEN transition. The assertion table in the trace itself marks this as 'PARTIAL'. Because the expectation is about a GREEN phase (which requires a preceding RED phase), and no such phase structure exists, this fails.

expectations.text: An adversarial review step is performed on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Perform Adversarial Review' explicitly lists missing checks: no verification that tests actually verify behavior, no edge case gap analysis, no check for subtle implementation bugs that could pass tests, no anti-pattern detection. No adversarial review of any kind occurs.

expectations.text: A tdd-report.md or equivalent summary is generated at the end
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 7 says Claude provides only 'a conversational summary' listing files and snippets. Section 'Does NOT Produce a Structured Report' confirms: 'Default Claude gives a conversational summary. It does not produce a structured report with per-work-unit RED/GREEN status, phase execution logs, test coverage metrics, anti-pattern scan results, adversarial review findings.' No file is generated.

expectations.text: The skill does NOT skip any verification steps even for simple specs
expectations.evidence: All verification steps are absent. The trace documents 11 distinct categories of behavior that default Claude does NOT perform, including: no RED phase, no GREEN verification cycle, no information barrier, no adversarial review, no spec compliance review, no anti-pattern detection, no design gate, no state management, no decomposition, no systematic framework detection, and no structured reporting. Every verification step is skipped.

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) — asks clarifying questions about the auth system before decomposing
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 explicitly triggered with all 3 conditions met (3+ features, external integrations, ambiguity). Three clarifying questions asked one-at-a-time: (1) password reset token expiry, (2) failed login lockout behavior, (3) registration email verification. Questions were asked BEFORE decomposition — the trace shows Phase 2 (decomposition) only begins after the design gate completes and user confirms. The hard gate blocked decomposition until user approval (trace line 141: 'HARD GATE: No decomposition proceeds until the user confirms').

expectations.text: Produces a design summary with components, data flow, and key decisions before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Design summary produced in Phase 0 Step 3 (trace lines 104-135) containing: 6 components (UserRepository, RegistrationService, LoginService, PasswordResetService, TokenService, EmailService), Data Flow section with 3 numbered flows (Registration, Login, Password Reset), Key Decisions section (JWT stateless, bcrypt hashing, lockout policy, reset token design, email service abstraction, in-memory storage), and Out of Scope section. Summary approved via hard gate before Phase 1 begins.

expectations.text: Decomposes into at least 3 work units (registration, login, password-reset or similar)
expectations.evidence: Phase 2 decomposes into exactly 3 work units: (1) user-registration (lines 180-186), (2) user-login (lines 188-194), (3) password-reset (lines 196-202). Each unit has a detailed spec-contract, dependency list, test file paths, and implementation file paths. The summary confirms '3 work units with dependency analysis' and the final report shows 3/3 completed.

expectations.text: Identifies dependencies between units (login depends on registration, password-reset depends on registration)
expectations.evidence: Dependency analysis explicitly shown in trace lines 204-215. user-registration has dependsOn: [] (no deps). user-login has dependsOn: ['user-registration'] (needs User type and repository). password-reset has dependsOn: ['user-registration'] (needs User type and repository). ASCII dependency graph drawn showing user-registration as root with user-login and password-reset as children. The session log confirms this: decomposition.complete event lists all three units with their dependency arrays.

expectations.text: Respects dependency order — implements registration before login and password-reset
expectations.evidence: Execution order explicitly documented: user-registration runs first as a complete RED->GREEN->review cycle (trace lines 281-743, marked as 'WORK UNIT 1'). Only after user-registration is marked completed (line 738: 'unit.completed') do user-login and password-reset begin in parallel (lines 747-751: 'WORK UNITS 2 & 3 (PARALLEL EXECUTION) ... both depend on user-registration (now completed) but NOT on each other'). The session log also confirms: test-writer.spawned for user-login and password-reset events appear after unit.completed for user-registration. The summary confirms: 'Sequential: user-registration ... Parallel: user-login, password-reset'.

expectations.text: Each work unit goes through the full RED→GREEN→review cycle independently
expectations.evidence: Each of the 3 work units follows the identical cycle: (1) Test Writer spawned -> tests written, (2) RED verification (5 checks: tests exist, tests fail, correct failure type, assertion density, behavior-over-implementation), (3) Code Writer spawned with information barrier, (4) GREEN verification (4 checks: checksum, no skip markers, all pass, no hardcoded returns), (5) Spec Compliance Review, (6) Adversarial Review. user-registration: lines 284-738. user-login: lines 754-940. password-reset: lines 946-1131. The final report table shows each unit with individual phase statuses (Test Writer, RED, Code Writer, GREEN, Spec Compliance, Adversarial Review).

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test across all units after individual completion
expectations.evidence: Phase 5 (Final Review, lines 1135-1194) runs the full test suite with 'npx vitest run' across all 3 test files simultaneously. Output shows 34/34 tests passing with exit code 0. Additionally, a cross-unit integration check (Step 4, lines 1178-1187) verifies: user-login depends on UserRepository/User from user-registration (compatible), password-reset depends on UserRepository/User from user-registration (compatible), and that a registered user can log in and reset passwords through the shared repository. The session log contains an 'integration.check' event with passed:true, totalTests:34, failures:0.

expectations.text: The report shows all work units with their individual phase statuses
expectations.evidence: The tdd-report.md (trace lines 1200-1298) contains three work unit sections, each with a detailed phase status table. User Registration (lines 1222-1239): Test Writer completed (11 tests, sonnet), RED passed (11 failures, 17 assertions), Code Writer completed (sonnet), GREEN passed (11/11), Spec Compliance compliant (6/6), Adversarial passed (4.6/5). User Login (lines 1243-1261): similar table with 12 tests, opus Test Writer, 20 assertions, score 4.5/5. Password Reset (lines 1265-1282): similar table with 11 tests, opus Test Writer, 16 assertions, 8/8 requirements, score 4.4/5. Summary table shows aggregates: 3/3 units, 34 tests, ~53 assertions, 0 violations, 3/3 adversarial reviews passed.

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) — asks clarifying questions about the auth system before decomposing
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1 explicitly states: 'Would it ask design questions first? No. Default Claude Code does not gate on design review.' The trace lists seven questions that would NOT be asked (database choice, JWT algorithm, password policy, lockout thresholds, email transport, app type, token expiry). Summary confirms: 'Claude Code would not ask a single clarifying question. It would infer defaults for every ambiguous decision.' No design gate was triggered.

expectations.text: Produces a design summary with components, data flow, and key decisions before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Produces design summary with components, data flow, key decisions — No. No design document produced.' The trace shows Claude Code went directly from intake (Step 1) to technology detection (Step 2) to implementation (Step 3) with no intermediate design artifact. The 'What Default Claude Code Does NOT Do' section under '1. No Design Gate' confirms: 'No data flow diagram or component identification.'

expectations.text: Decomposes into at least 3 work units (registration, login, password-reset or similar)
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Decomposes into at least 3 work units — No. No formal decomposition; features built linearly.' While Claude Code did implement three features (registration in Step 3c, login in Step 3d, password reset in Step 3e), these were not formalized as discrete work units with acceptance criteria, inputs/outputs, or tracked status. The 'No Formal Decomposition' section confirms: 'No work unit definitions with clear inputs, outputs, and acceptance criteria. No dependency graph. No tracking of unit completion status.'

expectations.text: Identifies dependencies between units (login depends on registration, password-reset depends on registration)
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Identifies dependencies between units — No. Dependencies are implicit in build order only.' The 'No Formal Decomposition' section confirms: 'No dependency graph (e.g., "login depends on registration because it needs user lookup"). No parallelization analysis.' Dependencies exist in the code (login needs a registered user) but are never explicitly identified or documented.

expectations.text: Respects dependency order — implements registration before login and password-reset
expectations.evidence: The trace shows registration (Step 3c) was implemented before login (Step 3d) and password reset (Step 3e), so the natural ordering is correct. However, the trace evaluation table rates this as only 'Partially' met, noting the order is 'by convention, not tracked formally.' There is no explicit dependency analysis driving the order. Grading as FAIL because the ordering is incidental rather than the result of dependency-aware planning, which is what the expectation describes ('Respects dependency order' implies a deliberate mechanism). The trace itself only awards 0.5 partial credit across all 8 assertions.

expectations.text: Each work unit goes through the full RED→GREEN→review cycle independently
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Each work unit goes through RED->GREEN->review cycle — No. No RED phase; tests written after all code.' Step 4 confirms: 'Default Claude Code writes tests after implementation is complete. The tests are shaped by what was built, not by what was specified.' The 'No RED/GREEN Verification' section adds: 'There is no RED/GREEN/REFACTOR cycle. Tests are never run in a failing state to confirm they detect missing functionality.' All three features were implemented monolithically before any test was written.

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test across all units after individual completion
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Runs final integration test across all units — No. Only runs jest once at the end; no dedicated integration pass.' Step 5 shows a single 'npx jest --verbose' run that executes all tests together, but this is not a deliberate integration test phase — it is the only test run, occurring after all implementation and test-writing is complete. There is no separation between unit tests and integration tests, and no cross-feature integration scenarios.

expectations.text: The report shows all work units with their individual phase statuses
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Report shows all work units with individual phase statuses — No. Output is conversational text, not a structured report.' Step 6 confirms the final output is: 'a conversational summary ("I built an auth system with these endpoints..."). This summary is unstructured text, not a formal report.' The 'No Structured Report' section adds: 'No work unit status table. No coverage metrics breakdown per feature. No spec compliance matrix.'

expectations.text: Detects that the user provided an existing test file (entry point mode 3)
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 2 explicitly identifies Mode 3 via the phrase 'implement against src/__tests__/calculator.test.ts' matching the SKILL.md line 102 trigger: 'User says "implement against this test..." or provides a failing test file'. The trace sets entryPoint = 'user-provided-test' in state. The summary confirms: 'The phrase "implement against" combined with a test file path triggers Mode 3 (User-Provided Test) per SKILL.md line 103.' This matches the actual SKILL.md line 102-103 which defines Mode 3.

expectations.text: Skips the Test Writer phase — does NOT rewrite or replace the user's test file
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 3b states: 'SKILL.md line 114: Mode 3 skips "Test Writer phase entirely." The Test Writer agent (defined in reference/test-writer-prompt.md) is never spawned. No teammate prompt is constructed.' Verified against actual SKILL.md line 113-114 which reads: 'Mode 3 (User-Provided Test) skips: - Test Writer phase entirely'. The summary table confirms 'Test Writer | SKIPPED | Mode 3: user-provided test'. The trace proceeds directly from framework detection to Code Writer with no Test Writer step.

expectations.text: Reads the existing test file from disk and passes contents to the Code Writer
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 9 explicitly states: 'The orchestrator reads the test file contents FROM DISK: Read file: src/__tests__/calculator.test.ts'. The Code Writer prompt template shows '<contents of src/__tests__/calculator.test.ts read from disk>'. The information barrier checklist at the end of Step 9 confirms: '[x] Prompt contains test file contents read from DISK (not from any previous agent output)'. This aligns with SKILL.md lines 304-305: 'The Code Writer prompt must contain ONLY: - The test file contents (read from disk, not from Test Writer output)'.

expectations.text: Still performs GREEN verification (checksum check, all tests pass)
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 10 is titled 'GREEN Verification (Phase 4, Step 4d) — RUNS FULLY' and states: 'GREEN verification is NOT skipped in Mode 3.' It details all four GREEN checks: (1) test file checksum comparison against stored checksums, (2) skip/focus marker grep, (3) all-tests-pass execution, (4) hardcoded return heuristic. The trace also includes Step 8 which records checksums before the Code Writer runs, establishing the baseline for GREEN Check 1. This is consistent with SKILL.md lines 319-327 and anti-cheat.md lines 165-228 which define GREEN verification with no Mode 3 exemption.

expectations.text: Still performs adversarial review on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 12 describes the full adversarial review process: 'The Adversarial Reviewer teammate is spawned with: spec-contract, test file contents, implementation file contents, and the scoring rubric.' It lists all 5 evaluation categories (Test Completeness, Test Quality, Implementation Quality, Cheating Detection, Coverage Gaps) and the 5 anti-patterns from testing-anti-patterns.md. The summary table confirms 'Adversarial Review | Full 5-category review with cheating detection and anti-pattern scanning'. SKILL.md lines 351-372 confirm adversarial review has no Mode 3 exemption.

expectations.text: Does NOT ask the user to confirm a work decomposition plan for a single provided test
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 6 states: 'In Mode 3, the user provided exactly one test file. The decomposition is trivial: Single work unit.' It then explicitly addresses confirmation: 'No confirmation dialog for a single test file: The SKILL.md lines 203-229 show the confirmation flow with a multi-unit work plan. With a single test file in Mode 3, there is no meaningful decomposition to confirm — the user already specified exactly what to implement. The "Proceed? [confirm/modify/cancel]" dialog would be redundant noise for a single file the user explicitly named.' However, this is an inference by the trace author, not an explicit rule in SKILL.md — SKILL.md lines 201-232 show the confirmation flow without a documented exception for single-unit cases. The behavior is reasonable but the justification relies on interpretation rather than explicit skill text.

expectations.text: Detects that the user provided an existing test file (entry point mode 3)
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states: 'Default Claude Code has no concept of modes. It just reads the file and writes code.' and 'Detect this as "entry point mode 3" (user-provided test) -- it has no mode concept' under 'What Claude Code would NOT do'. Summary confirms: 'FAIL -- no mode concept'. The baseline treats the prompt as a generic natural-language instruction with no structured mode detection.

expectations.text: Skips the Test Writer phase -- does NOT rewrite or replace the user's test file
expectations.evidence: Trace rates this PARTIAL: 'Claude Code would not proactively rewrite the tests, but it has no hard protection against modifying them under pressure.' Specifically, the trace warns Claude Code may 'Adjust assertions it believes are "incorrect"', 'Change import paths', 'Add .skip or .todo to problematic tests', or 'Rewrite test descriptions' if the implementation proves difficult. There is no Test Writer agent to skip (vacuously true), but the key protection -- treating the user's test file as an immutable contract -- is absent. The lack of checksum enforcement or skip-marker detection means test file integrity is not guaranteed. Graded FAIL because the assertion requires the test file to be reliably preserved, not merely likely preserved.

expectations.text: Reads the existing test file from disk and passes contents to the Code Writer
expectations.evidence: Trace confirms the file is read: 'Claude Code WOULD read the test file. When a user references a specific file path, Claude Code reliably reads it.' However, the assertion implies the TDD skill's architecture: a separate Code Writer agent receiving the test contents through an information barrier. The trace states: 'there is no separate Code Writer agent and no information barrier. The same context that reads the tests also writes the implementation.' Summary confirms: 'PARTIAL -- reads file, but no separate agent'. Graded FAIL because the pass-to-Code-Writer component is entirely absent; reading the file alone does not satisfy the structured handoff this assertion describes.

expectations.text: Still performs GREEN verification (checksum check, all tests pass)
expectations.evidence: Trace states: 'No checksum verification whatsoever. If the test file was modified, nobody would notice.' For test execution: 'Claude Code would LIKELY run the tests after writing the implementation' but 'this is NOT guaranteed' -- it may 'Skip running tests if it judges the implementation to be obviously correct' or 'Say "the tests should pass now" without actually running them.' The trace also notes 'No check for newly added skip/ignore markers in tests.' Summary confirms: 'FAIL -- no checksum; test run likely but not enforced'. Both components of GREEN verification (checksum integrity and mandatory test pass) are absent or unreliable.

expectations.text: Still performs adversarial review on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Trace is unambiguous: 'Default Claude Code performs no independent review. The same agent that wrote the code would need to critique it -- a fundamental conflict of interest. No anti-pattern detection, no scoring rubric, no structured review output.' Summary confirms: 'FAIL -- no independent review'. There is no separate reviewer agent, no edge-case analysis, no cheating detection, and no structured evaluation of the test+implementation pair.

expectations.text: Does NOT ask the user to confirm a work decomposition plan for a single provided test
expectations.evidence: Trace states: 'Default Claude Code would also not ask for a work decomposition plan because it has no concept of work decomposition. It would just start implementing. This is coincidentally correct behavior for this assertion.' Summary confirms: 'PASS -- coincidentally correct'. The baseline proceeds directly to implementation without prompting for plan confirmation, satisfying the assertion even though the reason differs from the TDD skill's intentional design.

expectations.text: Detects entry point mode 2 — existing codebase coverage mode
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 3 explicitly shows the skill matching 'add test coverage for src/utils/' against Mode 2 ('existing-codebase'). The trace shows the three-pattern decision table and confirms Mode 2 matches. Summary line 9 confirms: 'Sets entryPoint = "existing-codebase"'. This is also corroborated by SKILL.md lines 96-103 which define the entry point detection patterns, and line 106 which begins Mode 2 adaptations.

expectations.text: Reads and analyzes the existing source files in src/utils/ before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 7b ('Read and analyze each source file') describes the orchestrator reading each file with 'cat src/utils/stringHelpers.ts' and extracting exported function signatures, parameter/return types, observable behavior, and edge case handling. A concrete example analysis is shown for stringHelpers.ts with capitalize(), slugify(), and truncate() -- including specific behavioral descriptions and edge cases (empty string, maxLen=0, etc.). Step 7c shows these analyses becoming 'reverse-engineered spec-contracts' that feed the Test Writer. Summary lines 19-21 confirm: 'The orchestrator reads each source file in src/utils/ to produce reverse-engineered spec-contracts.'

expectations.text: Skips the design gate because --skip-design flag was passed
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 5 shows Phase 0 is entirely skipped, with two independent skip conditions: (1) the --skip-design flag parsed in Step 1 (SKILL.md line 133), and (2) Mode 2 auto-skips the design gate (SKILL.md line 107). The trace correctly notes both conditions independently cause the skip, and that '--skip-design' is now redundant for Mode 2. Result: 'Phase 0 is entirely skipped. No clarifying questions, no design summary, no user approval gate. designSummary is set to null.' Summary lines 12-16 confirm this with the same dual-skip explanation.

expectations.text: Generates tests derived from reading specific source file contents (function signatures, return types, edge cases) — not just generic tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 9a shows the Test Writer receiving a prompt populated with specific function signatures and behavioral descriptions extracted from reading the source: 'capitalize(str: string): string' with behavior 'Returns input string with first character uppercased, rest lowercased' and edge cases 'empty string returns "", single char works'; 'slugify(str: string): string' with behavior about lowercasing, replacing spaces, stripping non-alphanumeric; 'truncate(str: string, maxLen: number): string' with specific edge cases like maxLen=0 and negative maxLen. The report (Step 12) shows per-unit test counts (e.g., 8 tests/16 assertions for string-helpers) with function-specific coverage. These are clearly derived from reading the actual source, not generic templates.

expectations.text: Describes or resolves the tension between RED verification and existing implementations (characterization tests pass immediately against existing code)
expectations.evidence: The trace addresses this tension explicitly in multiple places. Step 9b opens with: 'This is the key difference from iteration-1' and quotes SKILL.md lines 283-288 describing the adapted procedure. The summary (lines 25-39) dedicates a full section titled 'Coverage Mode RED Verification (The Iteration-2 Fix)' that states: 'The hide-and-restore approach preserves anti-cheat intent (proving tests are not tautological) while accommodating existing implementations. This resolves the iteration-1 tension where literal application of "tests must fail" would cause an infinite retry loop in coverage mode.' The verified source file anti-cheat.md line 122 explicitly states: 'Standard RED verification requires tests to FAIL. In coverage mode, the implementation already exists, so well-written characterization tests PASS immediately. This is expected -- not an anti-cheat violation.' The tension is not just described but resolved with a concrete mechanism.

expectations.text: Demonstrates the adapted hide-and-restore RED verification procedure for coverage mode (temporarily hide impl, verify import failures, restore, verify pass)
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 9b provides the full four-step procedure with exact bash commands: (1) Hide: 'mv src/utils/stringHelpers.ts src/utils/stringHelpers.ts.tdd-hidden'; (2) Run tests and verify failure with import errors: 'npx vitest run src/__tests__/stringHelpers.test.ts 2>&1; echo EXIT_CODE:$?' expecting 'Cannot find module'; (3) Restore: 'mv src/utils/stringHelpers.ts.tdd-hidden src/utils/stringHelpers.ts'; (4) Run tests again and verify pass: same test command expecting exit code 0. The trace also describes failure handling for each step (tautological tests if Step 2 passes, mischaracterization if Step 4 fails). I verified this matches the actual SKILL.md lines 283-288 and anti-cheat.md lines 120-162 which contain the same four-step procedure with .tdd-hidden suffix, success/failure criteria, and re-prompt messages. The report table in Step 12 shows 'RED Verification | passed (hide-and-restore)' for all four work units.

expectations.text: Produces a report showing coverage additions
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 12 shows the complete tdd-report.md contents. The summary table shows: 4/4 work units completed, 28 tests written, 56 assertions, 0 anti-cheat violations, 4/4 adversarial reviews passed. Each work unit section details: Test Writer results (e.g., '8 tests, 16 assertions'), RED verification status ('passed (hide-and-restore)'), Code Writer status ('skipped (no-op)'), GREEN verification, spec compliance, adversarial review findings, and files created (test files listed as new, impl files listed as 'pre-existing, unchanged'). The report explicitly notes entry point as 'existing-codebase' and includes an anti-cheat log section confirming hide-and-restore verification. The session log (tdd-session.jsonl) is also described with event types covering the full lifecycle.

expectations.text: Detects entry point mode 2 — existing codebase coverage mode
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1 states: 'No skill is invoked. No structured pipeline begins. No entry point mode detection occurs. Claude does not classify this as "coverage mode" or any other formal mode.' The summary confirms: 'No mode classification exists in default Claude.' Default Claude treats the input as natural language and has no concept of mode routing.

expectations.text: Reads and analyzes the existing source files in src/utils/ before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 2 documents that Claude reads each source file: Read('src/utils/stringHelpers.ts'), Read('src/utils/dateUtils.ts'), Read('src/utils/validators.ts'), Read('src/utils/apiClient.ts'), plus checks package.json for the test runner. The trace acknowledges the reading occurs but qualifies it as 'shallow' — no structured analysis, no spec-contracts, no dependency mapping, no work-unit decomposition. The assertion as worded ('reads and analyzes') is satisfied at a basic level.

expectations.text: Skips the design gate because --skip-design flag was passed
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1: 'The --skip-design flag has no semantic meaning to default Claude — it is either ignored entirely or loosely interpreted.' Assertion analysis in the trace: 'Default Claude has no design gate concept. There is no gate to skip. The absence of a design phase is incidental to default behavior, not a response to the --skip-design flag. Claude would behave identically with or without the flag.' The flag is not parsed or acknowledged; the behavior is incidental, not causal.

expectations.text: Generates tests derived from reading specific source file contents (function signatures, return types, edge cases) — not just generic tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 3 describes how Claude generates tests referencing actual function names (capitalize, slugify), actual parameter types, and actual return values. The tests are not generic smoke tests. However, the trace qualifies this heavily: 'tests mirror internal branches rather than specifying expected behavior independently,' and 'Edge cases are copied from visible code paths, not reasoned about from the function contract.' Granting a pass because the assertion explicitly asks for tests derived from 'reading specific source file contents (function signatures, return types, edge cases)' which is what implementation-coupled generation does — it reads specific contents and derives tests from them, even if the derivation method is not behavioral.

expectations.text: Describes or resolves the tension between RED verification and existing implementations (characterization tests pass immediately against existing code)
expectations.evidence: Trace assertion analysis: 'Default Claude has no concept of RED verification. It does not recognize the tension between "tests should fail first" and "implementation already exists." It does not mention, describe, or resolve this tension. It simply writes tests that pass and moves on.' The summary adds: 'No concept of RED verification or the tension it creates in coverage mode.' There is zero discussion of this conceptual challenge anywhere in the baseline behavior.

expectations.text: Demonstrates the adapted hide-and-restore RED verification procedure for coverage mode (temporarily hide impl, verify import failures, restore, verify pass)
expectations.evidence: Trace assertion analysis: 'Default Claude would never: 1) Temporarily rename/move implementation files, 2) Run tests to verify they fail with import errors, 3) Restore the implementation files, 4) Run tests to verify they pass with the implementation present. This four-step verification procedure is entirely absent from default Claude Code behavior.' The summary identifies this as the single strongest differentiator: 'entirely absent without the skill; a novel procedure that proves tests are not tautological when implementation pre-exists.'

expectations.text: Produces a report showing coverage additions
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 5 describes the output as 'a conversational summary' with a bulleted list. The trace explicitly lists what is absent: 'No assertion density metrics, No coverage percentage or coverage gap analysis, No anti-pattern flags, No work unit breakdown with status, No session log or event timeline, No machine-readable format, No verification evidence, No checksums or anti-cheat data.' The summary confirms: 'Only informal conversational summary, no metrics or structured output.'

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) due to ambiguity
expectations.evidence: The trace explicitly evaluates three trigger conditions from the SKILL.md spec: (1) 3+ distinct features ('payments', 'credit cards', 'refunds'), (2) external integrations (payment processors), (3) high ambiguity ('like credit cards and stuff', 'idk the details yet'). All three conditions are met. The trace states 'Result: ALL THREE positive conditions are met. The design gate TRIGGERS.' This matches the SKILL.md rule 'Run the design gate when ANY of: spec mentions 3+ distinct features, spec involves external integrations, spec is ambiguous about data flow/ownership/error handling.' No --skip-design flag was present.

expectations.text: Asks clarifying questions about the payment system -- does NOT assume specific technologies or providers
expectations.evidence: Three clarifying questions were asked: (1) 'What types of payments do you need to support?' -- offers examples without prescribing. (2) 'For refunds: should the system support full refunds only, or also partial refunds?' -- clarifies scope. (3) 'How should this system interact with an actual payment processor? Should it define an abstract interface that any processor could implement, or do you have a specific provider in mind?' The trace explicitly notes: 'The skill does NOT say "Should we use Stripe?" or "I'll integrate with Stripe." It asks the user to specify, or proposes an abstraction.' The design summary's Out of Scope section lists 'Specific payment provider implementations (Stripe, PayPal, etc.)' confirming no provider was assumed.

expectations.text: Asks questions one at a time, not as a dump of 10 questions at once
expectations.evidence: The trace shows three separate clarifying question blocks, each followed by a simulated user response before the next question is asked: Question 1 (payment types) -> user responds -> Question 2 (refund scope) -> user responds -> Question 3 (integration strategy) -> user responds. The trace explicitly states: 'It does NOT dump all questions as a numbered list.' This matches the SKILL.md instruction: 'Ask one at a time -- do not dump a questionnaire.' Three questions falls within the SKILL.md guideline of '1-3 targeted questions about ambiguities.'

expectations.text: Does NOT start writing code or tests before clarifying the spec
expectations.evidence: The trace includes an explicit section titled 'HARD GATE' (Step 5) that states: 'No Phase 1 (framework detection) has run yet beyond what was needed for the design discussion. No Phase 2 (decomposition) has run. No work units have been created. No Test Writers have been spawned. No Code Writers have been spawned. ZERO lines of code or tests have been written. ZERO files have been created in the project directory.' Framework detection (Phase 1) only runs in Step 6, after the user says 'confirm'. Work decomposition (Phase 2) runs in Step 7, also after confirmation. Agent orchestration (Phase 4) runs in Step 9. This ordering matches the SKILL.md requirement: 'HARD GATE: No decomposition (Phase 2) until the design is approved.'

expectations.text: Proposes design approaches with trade-offs (e.g., Stripe vs custom, sync vs async processing)
expectations.evidence: The trace does NOT show the skill presenting explicit trade-off options with pros/cons. The SKILL.md specifies in the Design Refinement Process step 2: 'Propose approaches (when there are genuine trade-offs): Present 2-3 options with pros/cons.' The third clarifying question ('abstract interface vs specific provider') could be interpreted as presenting two approaches, but it does not include any pros/cons analysis. The design summary lists 'Key Decisions' with rationale, but these are stated as fait accompli, not as options for the user to choose between. For example, it states 'Abstract processor interface: Decouples business logic from any specific payment provider' without presenting the alternative (e.g., 'vs. direct Stripe integration: faster to build but locks you in'). The expectation specifically mentions 'Stripe vs custom, sync vs async processing' as examples of trade-off proposals -- neither pattern appears in the trace.

expectations.text: Waits for user approval of the design before decomposing into work units
expectations.evidence: The trace shows a clear sequencing: (1) Design summary presented at the end of Step 5, ending with 'Proceed with this design? [confirm/modify/cancel]'. (2) A 'HARD GATE' section explicitly stating the skill stops and waits. (3) 'User responds (simulated): "confirm"'. (4) Only then does Phase 1 (framework detection, Step 6) and Phase 2 (work decomposition, Step 7) proceed. The decomposition in Step 7 produces 4 work units derived from the approved design components (PaymentProcessor interface, Payment model, charge flow, refund flow) -- not from the original ambiguous spec. This matches SKILL.md: 'HARD GATE: No decomposition (Phase 2) until the design is approved.'

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) due to ambiguity
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states: 'No design gate exists' and 'No design gate: Claude would not produce a formal design summary and wait for user approval before proceeding. There is no hard gate.' (trace lines 85-86). The summary confirms: 'Ambiguity detection triggers design gate -- No -- vagueness filled with defaults.' The baseline has no concept of a Phase 0 design gate. The vague phrasing 'idk the details yet' is not detected as an ambiguity signal that should trigger structured clarification.

expectations.text: Asks clarifying questions about the payment system -- does NOT assume specific technologies or providers
expectations.evidence: Trace describes two likely paths: (1) 70-80% probability path where Claude 'skips clarification entirely' and 'Jump straight to implementation', silently assuming Stripe, TypeScript, REST API; (2) 20-30% path where questions are asked but in a batch dump. In both paths, Claude assumes specific technologies -- the trace states: 'Pick a payment provider (almost certainly Stripe, as it dominates training data)' (line 32) and 'Assume Stripe as the payment provider without asking' (line 73). Summary confirms: 'Technology-agnostic clarification -- No -- assumes Stripe, TS, REST'.

expectations.text: Asks questions one at a time, not as a dump of 10 questions at once
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly identifies the 'question dump' anti-pattern: in the less likely path (20-30%), Claude would deliver '8-10 questions at once' (lines 43-54 show a sample batch of 10 questions). The trace states: 'This is the "question dump" anti-pattern -- 10 questions at once rather than a structured, progressive clarification flow.' (lines 55-56). In the dominant 70-80% path, no questions are asked at all. Neither path asks questions one at a time.

expectations.text: Does NOT start writing code or tests before clarifying the spec
expectations.evidence: The trace's dominant path (70-80% probability) is: 'Jump straight to implementation' (line 28). Claude 'would interpret the vague request, make assumptions, and produce' a full payment service module (lines 29-36). Step 2 ('Assumed Implementation') describes the files and code Claude would generate without any clarification. The summary confirms: 'Claude would skip clarification entirely and jump straight to implementation.' Even in the minority path where questions are asked, there is no hard gate preventing code generation before answers are received.

expectations.text: Proposes design approaches with trade-offs (e.g., Stripe vs custom, sync vs async processing)
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states under 'What Would NOT Happen': 'No trade-off analysis: Claude would not present options like "Stripe vs. custom processor" or "sync vs. async payment processing" with trade-offs for each.' (lines 89-90). Instead, Claude 'Picks one approach silently' and 'Assume a specific architecture (service class pattern) without discussing alternatives' (line 74). The summary confirms: 'Design summary with trade-offs -- No -- single approach, no alternatives'.

expectations.text: Waits for user approval of the design before decomposing into work units
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states: 'No spec approval checkpoint: Claude would not wait for the user to say "yes, this design looks right" before writing code.' (lines 104-105). There is also 'No work unit decomposition: The entire payment system would be implemented in one pass, not broken into small, independently testable units.' (lines 95-96). The end state (Step 4) shows Claude delivering a completed module and only then asking 'Let me know if you want me to adjust anything!' -- post-hoc review rather than pre-approval. Summary confirms: 'Hard gate on user approval -- No -- proceeds without approval'.

notes: Iteration 2: validates coverage mode RED verification fix (commit 8581c5d)
Eval 4 improved from 83% (iter-1) to 100% (iter-2) — hide-and-restore procedure now documented
Eval 5 regressed from 100% (iter-1) to 83% (iter-2) — trade-off proposal embedded in question instead of separate step
All runs are trace-based (simulated execution), not live runtime
